title: Starter Character
description: A small example schema bundled with Bot Manager Kit. It defines a basic character — name, intro, greeting, personality, likes, goals, dialog examples — and is meant as a starting point for your own schemas. Copy this folder under BotSchemas/ to a new name, edit `properties` to taste, and your version will show up in the schema picker on next launch.
$comment: SCHEMA AUTHOR'S GUIDE: x-ui-* keywords drive the editor tree; x-export-* keywords drive the Copy Station. A field with no x-ui-panel (or x-ui-panel: 'hidden') is skipped in the editor; a field with no x-export-target is omitted from all exports. The kit's Copy Station has 4 fixed outputs: 'Intro', 'Greeting', 'Background', 'Dialog Examples'. Each exported field is assigned to exactly one. Required-by-the-kit system fields (id, lastModified) are kept hidden. additionalProperties: false rejects unknown fields on save — flip to true if you're iterating loosely.

id.description: Unique identifier (UUID). Generated by the kit when a bot is created.
id.$comment: Required by the kit. Hidden from the editor and excluded from all exports.

lastModified.description: Last edit timestamp. Stamped automatically by the kit on save.
lastModified.$comment: Required by the kit. Hidden and not exported.

name.description: Display name shown on the Library card and the editor title bar.

intro.description: Paragraph description shown on the chat platform's bot card. Sets the reader's expectations for the character.
examples: A retired bounty hunter scratching out a living in a rain-soaked port town, hoping nobody from the old days finds her.

greeting.description: The very first message the bot sends when a user opens the chat.
examples: *She wipes down the bar without looking up.* "Pick a stool. I'll get to you when I get to you."

sex.description: How the character identifies. Free-form by default so the schema stays flexible.

personality.description: Personality, temperament, and demeanor. A paragraph or two of prose works well.
examples: Outwardly cheerful and quick with a joke, but slow to trust. Defaults to deflection when conversations get serious.

backstory.description: Character history, formative experiences, and motivations. Prose.
backstory.$comment: If you later want to split backstory into structured sub-fields (birthplace, family, formative_event), replace the body with `type: "object"` + `properties: { ... }` — the schema-driven editor will render a sub-section automatically.

goals.description: What the character wants — short-term, long-term, or both. Prose.

likes.description: Things the character is drawn to or enjoys.

dislikes.description: Things the character avoids, resents, or is unsettled by.
dislikes.$comment: Mirror of `likes`. To collect structured items (each with sub-fields), change `items` from `{"type": "string"}` to `{"$ref": "#/definitions/SomeBlock"}` and define the block under `definitions`.

dialog_examples.description: Few-shot conversations that show the character's voice. Each entry is one example, each line is a {speaker, line} pair.
dialog_examples.$comment: Array-of-arrays: outer is the list of examples, inner is the lines of one example. Most chat-bot platforms accept this shape (some flatten on the way out).

images.description: Filenames of images attached to this character. Managed by the kit's Images view.
images.$comment: Remove this field (and `profileImage`) if your schema doesn't need images. They're kept here because the Library card uses `profileImage` for thumbnails.

profileImage.description: Filename (drawn from `images`) used as the Library card thumbnail.

DialogLineBlock.description: One line in a dialog example.

speaker.description: Who's speaking. Usually the character's name or 'User'.

line.description: What they say. May include actions in *asterisks* or quotes.

$comment: EXTENDING THIS SCHEMA: add properties under `properties`, tag each with x-ui-panel (one of: text, textarea, number, stringList, dialogExamples, or hidden) and x-ui-section + x-ui-order to control where it appears in the editor tree. To export the field, add x-export-target (one of: Intro, Greeting, Background, Dialog Examples), optional x-export-order, optional x-export-label (defaults to the property name). The Copy Station will pick up new fields automatically — no kit code changes needed. Look at the GrokBot schema in bot-manager-desktop for a heavier worked example.
